data.title: TC Energy Or Enbridge: Comparing Their Key Financials
data.description:
Investment thesis

Investors interested in Canadian pipelines have a choice between the two major players: TC Energy Corporation (NYSE:TRP) and Enbridge Inc. (NYSE:ENB). Both are based in Calgary, Alberta, Canada and have American listings using the symbols above.

But how do their financials compare? Is there an advantage in owning one rather than the other? After reviewing their key financials, I consider Enbridge to be the better choice for new investors, although TC Energy has outperformed it in the past.

About TC Energy and Enbridge

Both operate in Canada and the U.S.A., while TC Energy extends into Mexico as well.

On its website, Enbridge claims, “We move about 30% of the crude oil produced in North America, we transport nearly 20% of the natural gas consumed in the U.S., and we operate North America’s largest natural gas utility by volume. Enbridge was an early investor in renewable energy, and we have a growing offshore wind portfolio.”

TC Energy describes itself this way in the About section of its Q1-2025 earnings report, “Out extensive network of natural gas infrastructure assets is one-of-a-kind. We seamlessly move, generate and store energy and deliver it to where it is needed most, to home and businesses in North America and across the globe through LNG exports. Our natural gas assets are complemented by our strategic ownership and low-risk investments in power generation.”

TC Energy has a market cap of $51.91 billion, while Enbridge’s capitalization totals $97.94 billion.

Both firms recently released their first-quarter-2025 earnings reports and those results are included in the analyses below.

Revenue growth

I’m showing revenue by chart to compare their growth profiles rather than the amount of revenue they collected. First, we see Enbridge’s revenue has fluctuated over the past five years, but has been on an upswing over the past four quarters.

Second, TC Energy’s revenue also has fluctuated, but has remained relatively flat for the past year:

Over the five years from Q2-2020 to Q1-2025, TC Energy’s revenue has risen from $9.50 billion to $9.65 billion or 1.5% over five years.

Over the same period, Enbridge’s revenue has risen from $32.35 billion to $42.35 billion, an increase of $10 billion or 30.91%.

Advantage: Enbridge

Profitability

Having revenue is one thing, but how effective has management been in turning that revenue into profits? We will compare the firms using net income margins and returns on equity over the past five years. We’ll start with TC Energy’s net margin:

Over the past half decade, TC Energy’s net margin has slipped from 34.40% in Q2-2020 to 32.25% in Q1-2025, a difference of 2.15%.

And although not as high, Enbridge’s net margin has nearly doubled:

Its margin rose from 5.23% in Q2-2020 to 10.33% in Q1-2025, an increase of 97.51%.

On this metric, do we prefer a big increase or a consistently higher return with no growth? I pick the latter, and give the advantage to TC Energy.

There is another important way to measure profitability: return on equity, or ROE, which is often used by investors. This is the chart for Enbridge:

In the past two quarters, its ROE has grown, but that only gets it back up to a couple of previous highs.

TC Energy grew its return since the fourth quarter of 2023, and then essentially leveled out:

At 12.74, TC Energy’s ROE is 34.67% higher than Enbridge’s at 9.46, and has the advantage in this contest.

Earnings growth

This five-year chart compares the basic EPS of the two firms:

TC Energy shows more strength on this chart. Its basic EPS of $2.92 is 54.50% greater than Enbridge’s $1.89, despite the latter having much higher revenue.

Looking ahead, Wall Street analysts expect TC Energy’s normalized EPS to shrink by 0.02% this year, and over the next three years they anticipate growth averaging 2.0% per year. The analysts have a stronger estimate for Enbridge this year, 6.81%, and over the next three years expect average growth of 7.64% per year.

Over the past five years, TC Energy has been the better performer, while future estimates favor Enbridge. Presumably, if the latter grows more quickly than the former over the next few years, it will narrow or close the historical gap.

Dividends

Even though its share price has risen by more than 22.0% in the past year, Enbridge has a forward dividend yield of 6.01% (as of Tuesday, May 20, 2025, when the share price closed at $45.61).

TC Energy’s share price has risen 27.40% in the past year, and with the price closing at $50.06 on May 20, it has a yield of 4.92%.

Both have high payout ratios, with Enbridge at 122.98% and TC Energy at 87.67%.

Dividend growth: Enbridge’s has gone up by 5.37% in the past five years while TC Energy’s has grown 6.38%.

Although there are minor differences between the two, I would judge them as equal in a comparison.

Debt levels

Capital intensity is very high in the pipeline and storage industry. Not only are funds needed for pipe and construction costs, but also for land acquisition, and, increasingly, consultations and lobbying with multiple stakeholders.

To deal with these costs, firms in the industry typically use a lot of equity and debt capital. Therefore, investors will want to know the debt-risk profiles of these firms.

On a total debt/equity basis, TC Energy has less risk than its counterpart:

On another front, their interest coverage ratios, both do well. Enbridge’s current ICR is 5.92 and it has averaged 6.88 over the past half-decade. During that same period, TC Energy’s averaged 4.75, while its current ratio is 4.52. Any ratio above 4.0 is strong, so consider them both robust.

Judging by the total debt/equity ratio, TC Energy has the edge.

Cash flow

In 2021, Enbridge began increasing its free cash flow per share while TC Energy’s declined. Then they both flipped direction in the first quarter of 2024:

But, TC Energy wasn’t able to maintain that momentum in the first quarter of 2025, leaving investors wondering if this is a temporary setback or the beginning of a new decline.

Free cash flow usually allows companies, in general, to repurchase shares and make capital expenditures. Neither company has bought back shares in 2024, although Enbridge did make some repurchases in 2023 and 2022.

On the capital expenditures side, Enbridge increased its spending, or investment, from $4.230 billion in 2019 to $4.668 billion in 2024, an increase of 10.35%. On the other hand, TC Energy reduced its spending from $6.301 billion to $4.395 billion between 2019 and 2024, a decrease of 30.25%.

Given that greater capital expenditures usually lead to higher revenue and (hopefully) higher earnings, I’ll assign the advantage for free cash flow to Enbridge.

Total returns

For shareholders, what matters is the ultimate return. How much are they receiving from capital gains and dividends?

As this 10-year chart makes clear, TC Energy comes out significantly ahead of Enbridge:

There was rough parity between the two companies in 2015, but TC Energy has solidly outperformed Enbridge on total returns since then.

Advantage: TC Energy

Past performance score:

On the basis of metrics measuring past performance, TC Energy has the advantage four times, while Enbridge has it twice:

  • Revenue growth: Enbridge
  • Net income margin: TC Energy
  • Return on equity: TC Energy
  • Dividends: tied
  • Debt/equity: TC Energy
  • Interest coverage: both strong
  • Free cash flow: Enbridge
  • Total return: TC Energy

These results are unweighted, and if were to weight them, I would give total return perhaps half the score and split the remainder among all other metrics. A weighted choice again leads me to TC Energy.

But, how does it fare when we look ahead?

Future performance

Enbridge’s revenue has grown over the past four quarters, while TRP’s has remained flat. The former has more momentum going into Q2-2025 and the rest of the year than the latter.

Enbridge's net margin also has grown while TRP’s slipped slightly, but at the end of Q1-2025, TRP’s margin is roughly three times as high as that of ENB.

I do not expect dividends to make a difference in forward assessments.

Earnings momentum also favors Enbridge, with growth of 6.81% expected this year, while TC Energy’s earnings are expected to decline 0.2%.

I don’t consider debt risk a factor going forward, since both have strong debt/equity and interest coverage ratios.

Increasing capital expenditures by Enbridge and contraction by TC Energy lead me to expect more of Enbridge in the future.

Total returns: On the chart above, note how TC Energy’s return is rising sharply, while Enbridge’s is rising gradually. If they continue on their respective trajectories, TC Energy has the edge. However, I also note that TC Energy’s chart is more volatile, making it more likely its path will turn down again in the not-too-distant future.

Conclusion

I believe Enbridge is a better choice for the future, although TC Energy has outperformed it in the past. Both are solid performers, with excellent dividends, and similar low-risk profiles.

But, with its higher revenue growth, earnings momentum, and higher capital expenditures, Enbridge seems better positioned to deliver higher earnings and capital gains in at least the near future.

On this basis, I rate TC Energy a Buy and Enbridge a Strong Buy

data.title: Seeking 13% Yields: Greystone Housing
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

I don't usually like to touch on taxes, because each person's tax situation is extremely unique. The best way to get proper tax advice is to sit down with a registered tax professional who can look at your situation directly and give you advice.

However, when it comes to income investing, one frequent detraction that people like to point out is that it's "less tax advantageous" than other types of investing, like growth. I would say that is partially true, with a massive caveat. You can definitely organize your taxation situation to your benefit by using various accounts, like a Roth IRA, IRA, or a taxable account, and placing your holdings within those various accounts effectively. Furthermore, is it really tax advantageous to have a bunch of unrealized gains that could vanish in a moment versus cash in hand and paying the tax man as you get it instead of when you hope to have a windfall gain in the future?

One way you can help maximize the dollars you get versus the dollars you pay Washington, or whatever government entity feels they should have their hand in your pocket, is by buying tax-advantaged income. This could be in the form of qualified dividend income from a C-Corporation. It could come in the form of tax-free income by buying municipal bonds or the funds that hold them. Today, we're going to look at an opportunity where you can buy a company that generates much of its income from tax-free sources, which then forwards it to you. Is the income 100% tax-free? No, but much of it is.

Let's dive in!

Closing the Gap With Income

Greystone Housing Impact Investors LP (NYSE:GHI), yielding 13%, is a publicly traded partnership that issues a K-1 at tax time. They focus on a two-pronged approach to generate revenue, which they reward unitholders with. Firstly, they invest in mortgage revenue bonds, which are issued by government organizations to support sustainable housing efforts. Secondly, they invest in building and leasing properties, which, once leased up, they sell for a profit.

GHI reported earnings with CAD (Cash Available for Distribution) of $0.31/unit. We prefer to use the CAD metric for GHI because ultimately, GHI seeks to distribute 100% of CAD and we want to own companies that are paying us healthy distributions.

In 2024, GHI produced CAD of $0.95 for the entire year in 2024. GHI's best quarter in 2024 was $0.27, so $0.31 is starting off on the right trend.

GHI's core investment strategy is interest rate sensitive. Rising interest rates have been a headwind for GHI. In 2024, GHI entered into interest rate swaps, which limited the impact of interest rate changes. Currently, every 100 bps decline in interest rates will improve GHI's earnings by $0.052/unit: Source

This core strategy involves investing in MRBs (Mortgage Revenue Bonds), which are mortgages that are issued by state housing agencies. GHI uses leverage on this trade, and profits from the spread between what they pay to borrow and what they receive on the mortgage.

This business will produce more earnings as GHI can either borrow at a lower interest rate if SOFR declines, or as GHI is able to replace maturing mortgages issued when rates were lower with higher-coupon mortgages.

The MRB strategy provides GHI with recurring cash flow, while GHI's JV development strategy provides upside. The JV strategy involves investing capital into the development of multifamily residential buildings, which are then built by the JV partner, leased up, and then sold to investors as a stabilized apartment building. This strategy can provide significant CAD, but it is inconsistent and lumpy. GHI went 6 quarters without any of these properties selling, which caused CAD to come in below the distribution in 2024.

2025 is off to a better start, with one property sold in January and a second property sold in May. The property in January sold at a minimum price, resulting in GHI getting their guaranteed preferred return, but no excess for CAD. The property sold in May will result in $0.08 in CAD.

It is worth noting that the property was purchased by a non-profit which financed the property with two MRBs. GHI acquired the MRBs as part of their compensation, at a discount to par.

GHI will now be collecting interest on this property through the MRB portion of their business.

2025 is off to a much better start than 2024 in terms of earnings, even as its share price is lower. In one sense, the lower price made sense with the risk that the distribution might be lowered. We even wrote in our coverage last quarter:

"We would love to sit here and tell you that there is no way GHI will cut its distribution, but we can't do that. The development strategy makes up a substantial portion of GHI's capital, and GHI relies on that strategy to provide occasional lump sums of cash to fund the distribution."

Fast-forward, and GHI now has another property sale that did generate some CAD. We also see lending activity picking up from commercial mortgage REITs, which suggests that the commercial real estate market is thawing out. Properties are starting to sell again, and that's a positive for GHI's development business.

The distribution can't be guaranteed, and the market is far from "hot", like it was in 2021 and 2022, leading to large special distributions from GHI. However, it is moving, and GHI is selling properties. This provides a lot more support for the distribution going forward and gives us more confidence that it is going to be sustained.

Conclusion

GHI generates primarily tax-free income from its MRB business. Its revenue from that is supported and boosted by its ability to sell the additional properties. Those additional property sales are not in the same tax-free bracket as their other business, meaning that when those additional windfalls do occur, they will receive higher levels of taxation. The biggest benefit of GHI is its vast portfolio of tax-free revenue-generating assets. From my personal perspective, I am holding GHI for the income that those assets generate and the access to that market, and anything extra is just that it's extra–wonderful and enjoyed, but not necessarily relied upon.

When it comes to retirement, your tax situation is going to be vastly different than your tax situation in your working years. You're no longer going to have an employer that gives you a W-2 unless you're working in your retirement years. This means that your taxes will be very different, and you may wish to visit a tax professional to help you fine-tune your approach. Regardless of whether you're working or retired, income is a must to survive in today's world. So, I strongly encourage you to create a portfolio that will generate the income that you need. This way, you never have to go without. Within High Dividend Opportunities, we use our unique Income Method to generate the income that our subscribers need from their portfolio. They have what they need, and they have extra.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: How To Build A $1,000 Monthly Stable Dividend Income
data.description:
Recently, a friend of mine approached me with a request for a recommendation on how to build an income-oriented investment portfolio where he could earn at least $1,000 on average in dividend income each month.

He is relatively young, in his thirties, and while he doesn't plan to spend the money on financing lifestyle, the money is set to be used to partially offset his mortgage payments, while keeping money invested, rather than paying down the low interest 1-2% on 30-year fixed mortgage. In a nutshell, the money will be reinvested, but in a different asset category.

There are few main things that stand out here:

  • There isn't any intention to re-pay the mortgage early due to the very low interest rate secured during the COVID-19 pandemic historic lows, with investments offering far more attractive ROR.
  • His age is a great factor here, being in early thirties means, we would ideally focus on lower than higher yields (if the capital allows), with more aggressive dividend growth for even better dividend distribution potential in the future, while the underlying businesses reinvest cash, buyback shares, enabling him to benefit from capital appreciation.
  • When paying down debt, stability is key. It isn't a must to earn each month $1,000, there can be some variability from one month to another, but all equities should have sound growing businesses, healthy balance sheets with low financial gearing and reasonable payout ratios to sustain downturns.

The first question here is, how much money is he going to need to generate this $1,000 in income each month?

The math here is straight forward, we simply divide the annual pre-tax distribution $12,000 by the yield that we are targeting:

Dividend Yield Capital Required
2.0% $ 600,000
2.5% $ 480,000
3.0% $ 400,000
3.5% $ 342,857
4.0% $ 300,000

Given that his budget is somewhere in the range of $350,000 - $450,000, ready to be deployed, we need to strive for 2.67% - 3.43% dividend yield in order to earn at least $12,000 in dividend income pre-tax.

How To Build $1,000/Month Dividend Income Portfolio

As I said above, in this portfolio-building section we will focus our efforts to make sure we hit the target dividend yield, but we place a high priority on businesses, that show above-average dividend growth and are growing their top & bottom line altogether.

We certainly, don't want to select stalwarts, that are showcasing balance sheet strength and dividend stability, but limit the capital appreciation of the underlying portfolio.

Likewise, the portfolio needs to exhibit at least some degree of diversification. Yes, many investors go with only REITs or MLPs, but in my opinion, for a person who has limited knowledge in financial markets, diversification is key for sleeping well at night, yet still keeping our selection limited to 10 securities, as I see that as a sweet spot.

We also need to consider the month of distribution, which wouldn't be a factor if we didn't strive for roughly $1,000/month distribution.

Here is the sample portfolio I would recommend to go with:

Name Ticker % Weight $ Allocation % Yield Dividend Income 5Y DGR
Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG) 30.0% $ 122,919 1.78% $ 2,187.96 11.08%
Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF™ (SCHD) 25.0% $ 102,433 3.91% $ 4,005.12 11.44%

Agree Realty Corporation

 (ADC) 7.5% $ 30,730 4.06% $ 1,247.63 5.53%

Realty Income Corporation

 (O) 7.5% $ 30,730 5.73% $ 1,760.82 3.62%
Visa Inc. (V) 5.0% $ 20,487 0.64% $ 131.11 14.77%
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) 5.0% $ 20,487 1.02% $ 208.96 13.63%
Texas Instruments (TXN) 5.0% $ 20,487 2.89% $ 592.06 9.17%
AbbVie Inc. (ABBV) 5.0% $ 20,487 3.53% $ 723.18 7.23%
MSCI Inc. (MSCI) 5.0% $ 20,487 1.26% $ 258.13 20.11%
PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) 5.0% $ 20,487 4.32% $ 885.02 7.25%
Total 100% $ 409,731 2.93% $ 12,000.00 10.48%

This portfolio would yield on a weight average-basis 2.93%, yet still have double-digit dividend growth, diversified across ETFs, REITs, Financials, Tech, Healthcare and Defensives.

Given the requirement we would need to achieve at least $12,000 in annual pre-tax dividend distributions, we would need to invest $409,731 to achieve that.

Yes, the initial capital required is relatively high (even exceeding the value of the house, on which the mortgage is), but this way we win in multiple ways. We keep the low 1-2% interest on mortgage, have an investment portfolio represented by financially strong companies and diversified ETFs, while collecting monthly income that we use against the mortgage, yet still benefit from underlying capital appreciation on both assets.

For instance, at 10.5% dividend growth, in the 10 years time, if the growth would remain at the same level, would result in a staggering 7.94% of yield on cost.

Of course, if the assumptions change, and for instance we would need to be more aggressive on the yield side, we could instead of allocating 30% to dividend growth-oriented VIG, go overweight (e.g. 40-50%) on higher-yielding SCHD.

At the same time, I included a low yield companies like Visa, Broadcom and MSCI (with some yields as lot as 0.64%). The reasoning is straight forward, these companies operate in secular growth markets, with low financial leverage and the growth appreciation potential is very realistic, despite some of the valuations being on the high end currently.

For those, in search for higher total yield, e.g. 4-5%, these low yielding companies could be exchanged for instance for JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) with 7.98% dividend yield, or MLPs, such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) with yield of 6.57%.

It all comes down to the available capital and the requirement of how much of monthly distribution does the investor require. In this instance, it's the blend of monthly distribution & underlying capital appreciation.

Now, from the monthly distribution standpoint, generally the most common month to pay dividends is March/June/September/December. Generally, January-February are months which are underrepresented, yet I tried to make sure there aren't too wide swings.

Even though the payment dates may different from one quarter/year to another, as you see below, in the peak months the distribution would come to ≈$1,300/month, whereas in the low distribution months, it would fall down to ≈$650/month, having to make sure appropriate liquidity planning is in place to not overspend in the better months.

If a specific monthly income is needed, monthly-paying companies like Realty Income and Agree Realty are the superstars as their distribution is relatively flat (perhaps with some dividend growth) from one month to another, soothing out the swings.

The more problematic part from the timing of distribution standpoint are the two ETFs, where I allocate up to 55% of the capital, because this causes large chunks of distribution falling into specific periods (e.g. SCHD pays dividends in March/June/September/December), instead of it being broken down over more months. Selecting individual companies, or allocating less % weight to ETFs would resolve this issue, but bring another one, of overconcentration into either a specific company or sector.

From my standpoint, if investing for income, stability and reliability of the distributions is of utmost importance, hence quality, dividend-oriented ETFs like VIG or SCHD should create the core of such portfolios, easily allocating 50-70% of the whole capital, as these proven to be resilient picks over market cycles, bouncing back from downturns and delivering competitive ROR.

I've included in the portfolio also the go-to dividend paying equities such as PepsiCo, AbbVie and Texas Instruments, even though few of them are facing issues such as GLP-1 drugs reducing demand for non-healthy snacks and sodas, whereas AbbVie faces patent cliffs and the TXN is heavily reliant on the industrial sector accounting for 40% of its revenue, yet I see their business models as durable, with attractive yields and dividend growth of anywhere from 7-9%.

For the portfolio I selected, I ran a performance back test, going back to 2015 and as you can see the portfolio would deliver 11.59% CAGR, even beating the broader markets performance of 11.35% over the duration, with dividends not reinvested. A $10,000 would turn into $27,832 over one decade, with a lower drawdown, compared to the broader market.

Certainly, the performance is skewed a bit by homeruns, like Broadcom was thanks to the AI gold rush, but that's just 5% allocation.

Here you can see also the sector allocation, leaning more towards tech, as expected, since the last decade performance was predominantly tech-driven:

However, what's more interesting is the growth of the income. Back in 2016, this portfolio would yield just 39% of what it yields today.

This shows you the power of compounding, and while the 2025 are just year-to-date data, the growth is set to continue.

Few Of Strategy Inefficiencies

Certainly, no investment strategy is without its risks.

While dividend-paying equities are great for earning extra income, for younger investors, focusing exclusively on more mature companies with defensive business models and stable distributions can over the long-run lead to sub-optimal returns as the distributed cash is pulled from the business, instead of being reinvested (ideally above at ROI above 10-12% of the market returns) and the money no longer compounds.

Just consider, that until recently, great businesses with strong cash flows, such as Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META) or Amazon (AMZN), none of them paid any dividends. If you focused exclusively on let's say equities with a yield higher than 1%, you would have missed all these once-in-a-lifetime success stories.

Another issue, is taxation. Receiving a passive paycheck might lead to extra taxes. E.g. if you pay 10-25% on dividends, you won't be left with $1,000, but $750-900 instead. If you instead invested your money in broader market ETF, where dividends are reinvested automatically, you wouldn't have to pay any taxes, until capital appreciation isn't recognized, perhaps after years of compounding.

Investor's Takeaway

Receiving a passive income, no matter if $1,000 or $2,500/monthly is ideal, no matter if the proceeds are used to finance one's lifestyle, or used to pay-off mortgage.

In this particular instance, where the proceeds are used to pay-off mortgage, we are winning on multiple fronts. We have low 1-2% interest rate on mortgage with appreciating underlying asset. We built durable 2.93% yielding portfolio, where the distributions are used to pay-off mortgage, while the portfolio appreciates and distributions in absolute terms increase from one year to another, perhaps allowing is to improve our lifestyle, or eventually buy another property (or reinvest back to portfolio).

Despite having some swings in the monthly distributions caused by high allocation to dividend-paying ETFs, I see this strategy as resilient and attractive if the capital is available to build the portfolio.

data.title: Viking Therapeutics: Weight-Loss Story Remains Intact
data.description:
In a rather shocking move, Viking Therapeutics, Inc. (VKTX) traded down to $20 on the recent market sell off. The once hot weight-loss biotech slumped despite generally positive data and a strong outlook. My investment thesis remains ultra-bullish on the stock after this major dip, though the sector has lost some luster.

Weight-Loss Drug Dip

The weight-loss drug sector has lost a lot of momentum in 2025. The leading drug stocks have slipped since a peak in 2024, pulling down the small biotechs trying to produce competing drugs.

What is important to understand is that Eli Lilly (LLY) and Novo Nordisk (NVO) have both already seen massive gains, still captured in the lower stock prices. As a primary example, Eli Lilly still trades above $720 after only crossing $150 back in 2020 and the market cap sits at $650 billion.

As much as the sector leaders have given up some of the peak valuations, the primary stock has still seen substantial gains. The opportunity for Viking Therapeutics is to capture some of the upside from those combined $1 trillion market caps, though these biopharmas aren't entirely focused on weight-loss drugs.

The market for weight-loss drugs hasn't been altered, though the access to the market has changed dramatically. As more supply has become available from the main drugs companies, the direct-to-consumer channel has opened, likely due to firms successfully compounding the drugs during the shortage period.

Novo Nordisk has NovoCare Pharmacy and the firm recently signed deals with Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) and LifeMd (LFMD) to provide virtual platforms for Wegovy. In addition, HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said that popular GLP-1 weight-loss drugs should cost U.S. consumers the same as the lowest amount paid in other countries, adding to the general concern that weight-loss drugs won't always command premium prices.

Focus Remains On Obesity Program

Viking Therapeutics entered the year after reporting positive data on both the VK2735 Subcutaneous and Oral weight-loss drugs. The main story to start the year is all about enrolling patients in the Phase 3 trial for the daily shot program and launching the Phase 2 trial for the daily oral version.

The drugs continue to compare very favorably against competition. The Phase 3 ACHIEVE-trial for the Eli Lilly oral GLP-1 medication orforglipron saw the highest doses leading to only a 7.9% weight-loss after 40 weeks.

Viking Therapeutics saw a quick enrollment in their Phase 2 trial for the oral version of VK2725 after the Phase 1 trial showed an 8.2% weight loss reduction after only 13 weeks. The strong tolerability of the drugs from Viking provides for the strong interest.

The Phase 2 oral trial quickly completed enrollment in late March. The Phase 2 study enrolled 280 patients, with the primary endpoint of the study measuring the percent change in the body weight from baseline after 13 weeks of treatment.

The timeline will support a data readout around late-July, right around the Q2 '25 earnings call. The stock should have a major catalyst with the readout of the Phase 2 oral results along with the enrollment of the Phase 3 study for the daily shot.

The company could face a void after these 2 pivotal events take place while waiting for the Phase 3 trial results. Viking Therapeutics already has very strong Phase 2 weight-loss numbers with a nearly 15% loss after only 13 weeks, while leading drugs on the market took far longer to only cross the 20% mark.

Novo Nordisk recently replaced the CEO due to lackluster stock performance. The company even made a deal with Septerna (SEPN) in a deal worth up to $2.2 billion in cash and future royalties for initial development programs directed at GLP-1, GIP and glucagon receptors in a sign of the true value of Viking Therapeutics.

Viking Therapeutics ended Q1 with ~$852 million in cash, with plans for this amount to cover the testing for the obesity program, along with the other clinical programs. The company recently announced a manufacturing deal with CordenPharma to eliminate the primary concern of the small biotech remaining independent regarding the need to build up expensive manufacturing facilities.

The biggest risk to the investment story is a cash squeeze. If the stock falls below the recent lows around $20, Viking Therapeutics would fall to a market cap of around $2 billion, where the need to raise additional cash quickly becomes very dilutive.

The small biotech burned about $51 million in cash during Q1 alone. The Phase 3 trial should boost costs in the 2H, so the cash balance looks impressive now, but the amount will quickly burn, and any delays could have severe impacts on the liquidity position of Viking Therapeutics.

Naturally, the prime reason for the stock slipping could be competition making their weight-loss drugs virtually worthless before the small biotech can even complete trials and obtain FDA approvals. In addition, the marketing machines of Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk could make uprooting these large biopharmas very difficult, even with a leading GLP-1 drug with better weight-loss metrics and tolerability.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Viking Therapeutics has been unreasonably hit despite the promising weight-loss drug picture not changing. Investors should use the weakness to load up on the stock, while being prudent to sell the stock on any dip below the recent lows.

data.title: Private Markets Playbook: Positioning For A New Reality
data.description:
The game plan for building more innovative, resilient portfolios in the face of uncertainty.

As the economic landscape continues to evolve—with heightened volatility in money markets, fluctuating inflation expectations, and tightening liquidity conditions—private markets are emerging as an essential component of modern portfolios. Investors are increasingly turning to private assets for the potential of enhanced returns, greater diversification, and insulation from public market swings.

Still, private investments are not without complexity. They require a long-term view, rigorous diligence, and careful planning—especially in today's environment. One of the most common pitfalls we see is investors entering the space without a clear strategy. Private investments are not one-size-fits-all. To truly unlock their value, investors need access to a diversified range of opportunities across private equity, venture capital, infrastructure, real estate, and private credit.

Success in private markets depends on more than just timing. It requires knowing where to look and who to partner with. It means being able to balance opportunity with liquidity. With the right playbook—and access to the right managers—investors can build more resilient portfolios and help clients meet their long-term goals, even amid uncertainty.

What's moving markets

Macro Volatility and Monetary Policy

Recent swings in money markets reflect deep uncertainty around the path of interest rates and inflation. Central banks remain cautious, and higher-for-longer rate scenarios are adding pressure to traditional fixed income. In contrast, private credit and real asset strategies are emerging as compelling alternatives, offering potentially higher yields and better risk-adjusted returns.

Tariffs and Trade Tensions

With the Trump administration signaling a return to protectionist trade measures, including the reintroduction of tariffs on key imports, global supply chains could face renewed disruption. While this creates headwinds for some sectors, it also presents opportunities for private capital to fund domestic infrastructure, manufacturing reshoring and supply chain modernization. Investors should be alert to how these dynamics may impact valuations, especially in sectors like industrials, logistics and technology hardware.

Regulatory Winds

A more business-friendly regulatory tone under the Trump administration may favor private sector expansion, with looser rules and lower taxes possibly boosting M&A activity. For private equity, this could translate into increased deal flow and new opportunities in both the middle and large-cap markets. Globally, regulatory divergence continues, with varied risks and opportunities across Europe, Asia and the Middle East.

Need for Security

Rising geopolitical instability and the growing reliance on digital infrastructure are leading to greater investment in cybersecurity, cloud resilience, and data sovereignty. Governments and corporations alike are prioritizing spending in this space, creating new avenues for private capital to fund innovation and infrastructure.

Alternative Liquidity Sources

While IPO markets remain choppy, secondary transactions—including GP-led continuation vehicles—are gaining traction as viable exit paths. These mechanisms are critical in maintaining flexibility and liquidity for investors, particularly in a tighter capital-raising environment.

The Playbook

How can investors position themselves to take advantage of these opportunities? The answer is to consider a meaningful allocation to private investments across different segments.

Private Equity

We see more scrutiny on consolidation strategies and growing potential in non-U.S. markets.

  • U.S. market: An increasingly pro-business climate in the U.S. will likely drive corporate activity. We believe taking a targeted approach focused on growth themes including reindustrialization, supply chain resilience and strategies at the intersection of government-private sectors, remains attractive.
  • Artificial intelligence and automation: The accessibility of AI, automation and new system integration tools should support consolidation strategies across service sectors, from autobody shops to accounting firms.
  • Global: Outside of North America, we see potential in less traditional centers of private capital formation, including Japan, Asia-ex China and the Middle East. In Japan, 2025 should reveal whether corporate deal flow matches the private capital funding flows.

With public markets shrinking, private equity buyouts provide investors access to a broader opportunity set, enhancing returns through operational improvements, strategic repositioning, and sector expertise.

Vic Leverett

Managing Director, Head of Alternative Investments

Buyout Bounce Back

European private equity buyouts have risen off their 2022 lows.

Invested capital by primary sector

How to play it

Private equity continues to attract investors seeking higher returns and broad diversification. It provides exposure to operational improvements and strategic repositioning of companies not presently in listed markets. Private equity also allows investors to emphasize emerging trends in their portfolios such as reindustrialization, the energy transition and AI.

Venture Capital

AI will drive more productivity and fresh opportunities across industry and defense.

  • AI adoption: AI will continue to move further into the mainstream with innovations such as AI agents and virtual collaborators. However, widespread acceptance also means growth is likely fully priced into financing rounds. As a result, we tend to favor earlier-stage investing.
  • Hardware and data ownership: AI-driven code generation is changing the economics of software development, shifting the competitive advantage toward data and hardware. Technology breakthroughs are poised to make hardware more capital-efficient and investable. Governments are also prioritizing related sectors, pairing private sector capital with expanded sources of non-dilutive funding—all of which will support a flurry of new companies.
  • Digitization: The digitization of physical industries and the momentum in robotics, autonomous systems, and defense technology will continue to unlock new innovations and revenue streams in these sectors. We maintain strong convictions in opportunities across cybersecurity, "immersion" themes, bio-health, industrial automation, and hard tech.

With companies staying private longer, venture capital broadens investor access to high-growth opportunities and complements small cap exposure in a shifting market landscape.

Samuel Pittman, Ph.D.

Managing Director, Co-Head of Strategic Asset Allocation

Higher Valuations

Early-stage U.S. companies have seen valuations return to 2021 levels.

Median pre-money and median post-money valuations

How to play it

With the number of public market companies shrinking and private companies increasing, venture capital has become essential for accessing early-stage innovation. Allocating to venture capital not only provides exposure to these transformative companies early on but also helps investors maintain broad small-cap diversification.

Private Real Estate

Private real estate is in a period of recovery, offering meaningful value in both listed and unlisted markets.

  • Relative value: When listed and non-listed real estate assets are combined, the disparate characteristics can offer investors tangible relative value to trade mispriced securities and build or buy a broader array of property types across markets.
  • Market stabilization: On a trailing three-year basis through year-end 2024, both listed and non-listed real estate underperformed equities by double digits. We expect this performance delta to narrow in upcoming vintages. With high volatility in rates, new construction has slowed meaningfully across sectors, allowing for occupancies to stabilize and rents to increase in existing assets.
  • Europe: Rates may not moderate as expected, putting continued pressure on assets previously financed in less restrictive capital markets. We anticipate private core real estate valuations to bottom out this year while capital competes with nearly 8% yields available in areas such as investment-grade corporate mortgage-backed securities. As European markets are more heavily banked, with no real CMBS market, we think any bank retrenchment could still impact European equity values disproportionately, extending prospects for debt and non-core strategies.

The current environment presents compelling entry points for long-term investors to gain access to the inflation-hedging properties of real estate in their portfolios.

Amneet Singh

Director, Asset Allocation Strategy

Transactions Rebound

North American private real estate transactions have ticked up over the past year.

Volume of transactions by sector

How to play it

Rental income from real estate typically keeps pace with inflation over the long term, making it an essential asset class today. While office real estate remains stressed post-COVID, sectors like logistics and data centers are well-positioned to benefit from AI-driven demand. Real estate is an essential exposure in a total portfolio solution.

Private Infrastructure

Private infrastructure is positioned for growth, driven by inflation-linked cash flows and demand in digital and energy sectors, with risks from oversupply and geopolitics.

  • Energy infrastructure: The growth in demand for energy continues to encompass one of the most important areas for private capital mobilization. Whether from renewable sources or traditional fuels, providing electrons to power-hungry economies around the world is a growing area of opportunity.
  • Digital infrastructure: With the advent of AI, acute deficits in the supply of both power and water to meet demand will need to be addressed. Data center demand is also high; although, it's worth noting the potential for a supply overhang at some point is not low. This will make general partner skills and manager selection more vital.
  • Geopolitics: For Europe, the push to stay competitive could lead to outsourcing a large portion of the AI tech stack to more energy-abundant regions. In addition, the path of the energy transition in Europe and a potential resumption of energy flows from Russia could also influence infrastructure investment.

Infrastructure is at the heart of global transformation, offering stability, opportunity, and resilience in a shifting landscape.

Pierre Dongo-Soria, CFA

Principal Asset Allocation Strategist

Rise in Renewables

Renewables make up an increasing share of power generation in North America.

Power generation by fuel source, as a percentage of total power generated

How to play it

The energy transition and the build-out of AI models are driving massive infrastructure investments that will add to mature infrastructure segments like ports and communication networks. Infrastructure, with its inflation-hedging properties and its importance to the global economy, is a natural choice for a strategic allocation.

Private Credit

Senior security lending and asset-backed strategies thrive in private credit, with evergreen funds offering flexibility.

  • Senior secured lending: Senior secured lending, which has first claim on cash flows, is preferred for both corporate and real assets. Since much of the private credit market is floating rate, elevated base rates mean lenders will continue to absorb more cash flow. Elevated financing costs start to place more pressure on junior facilities and lower-quality sponsor-backed borrowers.
  • Asset-based lending: Lending against specific assets (asset-backed and asset-based) is preferred to lending on broader enterprise operating income or EBITDA (cash flow lending). As the need for capital expenditure grows, we think more innovation is coming to fund assets in non-bank markets.
  • Evergreen vehicles: With uncertainty over the path of rates, we see funds just beginning their investment cycle (early in lock-up periods), with fresh capital having more flexibility and a strategic deployment advantage in upcoming vintages. While these structures offer greater liquidity, they are not meant to house longer duration non-performing or distressed strategies. Should the non-bank lending market be tested by an extended period of defaults, closed-end credit specialists and an active secondary market will be available to provide solutions. Therefore, diversification by strategy and outperformance tied to manager selection is essential.

Private credit has generated strong investment returns on par with equity, albeit with much lower volatility, making it an important slice in an efficient portfolio.

Keith Brakebill, CFA

Director, Senior Portfolio Manager, Fixed Income

Size Matters

Medium-sized funds are most common

Private debt funds by fund size, as a percentage of total funds

How to play it

The private credit market is growing rapidly, to where it is now a substantial portion of sub-investment-grade lending. A diversified strategic asset allocation that includes sub-investment-grade fixed income should augment listed high-yield bonds and bank loans with private debt.

The bottom line

In the months ahead, we believe private markets will be redefined by a changing regulatory backdrop, new security requirements and fluctuating liquidity dynamics. This should create a host of new opportunities, albeit with heightened complexities. By deploying every tool in the playbook, investors can capture high-growth opportunities and unlock value across emerging sectors and regions, fueling breakthroughs that shape the future. To wit, the resurgence in private markets is already underway.
