data.title: Capital Southwest: Latest Quarter Confirms Why This Is A Top Income BDC
data.description:
Introduction

Although Business Development Companies are not seeing the performance they saw from 2022 to 2024, they remain attractive investments for investors seeking income. With the economy slowing and recession fears picking up, investors may be left wondering how the sector (BIZD) will fare going forward.

I think it's possible several BDCs will experience dividend cuts over the next 6 to 12 months as a result of lower base rates impacting their overall portfolio yields. However, there are some I think could navigate successfully.

Capital Southwest (NASDAQ:CSWC) is one of those, and their latest quarter gave me even more confidence in their ability to navigate lower base rates and a potential economic downturn.

In this article, I discuss Capital Southwest's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I think this BDC is poised to continue their solid performance going forward.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Capital Southwest in an article titled: Long-Term Bullish, But Not Adding Here. As the title stated, CSWC continues to be a great long-term holding for income. But due to expected lower base rates, I thought many BDCs could see lower share prices, giving investors better entry points in the near future.

Since then, CSWC's share price is down nearly 9% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up 1% at the time of writing. During their Q3 earnings, CSWC delivered a solid performance with a beat on their top & bottom lines and solid dividend coverage of 110%.

They also took a more defensive posture, increasing their first-lien exposure to 89.1% from 87.6% at the end of 2023. Non-accruals also managed to tick down quarter-over-quarter from 3.5% to 2.7% on a fair value basis.

But with a slowing economy and uncertainty increasing, CSWC's non-accruals at cost of 5.2% were higher than I preferred and was something I thought investors should keep a close eye on going forward.

Impressive Close Out

Capital Southwest reported their Q4 earnings recently and closed out the fiscal year strongly despite net investment income declining from the previous quarter.

This amounted to $0.56, down from $0.63 in Q3 and $0.68 year-over-year. Part of this was due to lower base rates, which impact BDCs' overall portfolio yields. CSWC's weighted-average yield stood at 11.7%, down from 13.3% in the previous year's quarter.

Another contributing factor was a $0.05 expense related to the exit of their CEO according to management. On an adjusted basis, the decline was only $0.02 from the previous quarter.

But here's why I thought Capital Southwest's performance was impressive despite lower yields and lower Nll. Total investment income for the full-year grew double-digits year-over-year from $178.1 million to $204.4 million.

For the quarter, this amounted to $52.4 million, up slightly from $52 million the previous quarter and $1.0 million from Q1. This was a result of their solid portfolio growth from $1.5 billion to $1.8 billion, and higher interest income.

CSWC did see some unrealized and realized losses during the quarter. These were $10.3 billion but were offset by equity gains of $19.3 million.

In Q4, management made $146.2 million in new investment commitments and $3.8 million in co-investments towards their equity portfolio.

This was slightly lower than the previous year's quarter that saw them make $157.5 million in new investments and Q3's $317.5 million, likely attributed to economic uncertainty and higher base rates compressing spreads.

NAV

Capital Southwest saw some decent NAV growth during the quarter, a result of issuing shares (above NAV). This grew to $16.70, up from $16.59 the previous quarter. But was slightly down from the previous year's $16.77.

While I would have liked to see growth year-over-year, a slight decline shouldn't raise red flags amongst investors. A BDC's NAV over a shorter time frame could be impacted by dividend payments, debt repayments, and exiting certain investments during the quarter.

For context, CSWC exited nine total investments, but generated proceeds of $40.6 million and net realized gains of $5.1 million. However, over the long-term, NAV should see steady growth.

And this is important because a BDC's share price typically follows their NAV growth. The more growth, the more upside is likely.

Why I Have Confidence in the Dividend

With the decline in net investment income, CSWC failed to cover its base dividend of $0.58. But here's why I think their base dividend will likely remain safe going forward.

Management stated they had undistributed taxable income of $0.79, up from $0.68 the previous quarter, that they could use to offset a further decline from lower base rates. And management anticipates this to grow larger in the upcoming quarter as a result of strategic investment exits.

CSWC's equity portfolio continues to be a secret weapon for them. Management stated that going forward they expect to see solid liquidity from investment exits and harvested gains from their equity portfolio.

And lastly, Capital Southwest received their second SBIC license last month. This gives them additional access to up to $175 million of capital they could use to fund growth or potentially use for dividends if need be.

Capital Southwest also reconfirmed the payment of their $0.06 supplemental payable next month, which should also give investors confidence in management's ability to continue rewarding shareholders.

While dividend growth could slow and supplementals will likely be eliminated as base rates continue to go lower, I suspect CSWC's base dividend will remain relatively safe going forward. Who knows? Shareholders may even get an end-of-year special this year.

Low-Leveraged Balance Sheet

Their balance sheet also gives me confidence in CSWC's ability to navigate a potential downturn and lower base rates successfully. The BDC has no debt maturing until October of next year and one of the lowest debt-to-equity ratios amongst its peers.

Leverage was just 0.89x and they had liquidity of $384 million. For comparison, CSWC's leverage is well-below the peer average and even some of their larger, more popular peers like Ares Capital (ARCC), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), and Blue Owl Capital (OBDC).

See below:

ARCC: 0.98x

BXSL: 1.19x

OBDC: 1.09x

They also increased their revolver to $510 million for the second time in less than a year. To solidify their balance sheet strength, they received an upgrade from BBB- to BBB flat from Fitch. Here's what management had to say about this during earnings:

Finally, we recently had our BBB minus corporate ratings from both Moody's and Fitch reaffirmed, as well as our secure debt rating from Fitch upgraded from BBB minus to BBB flat. This year, we continued our long track record of producing steady dividend growth, consistent dividend coverage, and solid value creation. Despite a year in which our base rate, SOFR, shrunk by over 1%, we grew our regular dividends from $2.24 per share in fiscal year 2024 to $2.31 per share in fiscal year 2025, while paying an additional $0.23 per share in supplemental dividends.

Limited Upside

Capital Southwest's share price peaked around $27 roughly a year ago. And while the stock could see some upside according to Wall Street, I continue to rate them a hold and expect them to trade in the $21–23 range for the near term.

For investors looking to buy, I would take advantage of any share price weakness, similar to last month's Liberation Day when the share price fell below $18 briefly.

While that was likely a one-off event, lower base rates could result in a retraction in share price. While they currently trade at a premium above their net asset value due to their strong fundamentals, I think Capital Southwest is a better buy near the $19 and below level.

Risks

Although lower base rates will result in lower portfolio yields, thus impacting CSWC's bottom line, a slowing economy is still a risk, particularly for their borrowers. The BDC did manage to see a decline in non-accruals at fair value from 2.7% to 1.7% in the prior quarter. These were as high as 3.5% in Q2.

So, management has seemingly done a good job with non-accruals. However, economic uncertainty due to potential tariffs and a possible recession could cause these to increase in the coming quarters.

Moreover, payment-in-kind income did rise from the previous year's $3.051 million to $3.982 million. But the cash income percentage of their top line was solid at 94%. But going forward, investors should keep a close eye on this.

Bottom Line

Capital Southwest's latest quarter solidified them as one of the top BDCs who could potentially navigate a downturn successfully.

With their ample liquidity and additional sources of capital, I expect them to at least continue covering their base dividend should the economy continue to weaken in the coming months.

Moreover, their balance sheet also gives them flexibility as they have no debt maturing this year, and robust liquidity available.

While lower base rates and a slowing economy continue to be a risk, Capital Southwest's latest quarter, fundamentals, and additional sources of capital show why they remain one of the best BDCs to own for income-oriented investors.

data.title: Japanese Bond Rout Continues, While Disappointing PMI Weighs On The Euro
data.description:
Overview

Disappointing flash May PMI readings in Europe and the Asia Pacific helped the US dollar stabilize after yesterday's drop. Asian currencies, including the yen, have been unsettled by reports that in bilateral trade discussions with the US, exchange rates have been discussed. This, coupled with extensive unhedged dollar exposure, rocked several of the regional currencies in recent weeks. Yesterday's 1.6% surge of the South Korean won has been mostly, though not fully unwound today. The dollar has edged lower against the Taiwan dollar for the third consecutive session today, and the sixth time in the past seven sessions. The greenback is higher against all the G10 currencies today, but the Japanese yen, which has also been impacted by fx-related talks.

Following the sharp US equity losses yesterday, Asia Pacific bourses tumbled today. Indonesia was the notable exception, and the rupiah, incidentally, is the strongest currency in the world so far today, rising by about 0.4% against the US dollar. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is off nearly 1%. It would be the first back-to-back loss in the index in two weeks. US index futures are slightly firmer. Japanese bonds remain under pressure, and European benchmark yields are mostly have edged higher. After yesterday's surge, US two- to 10-year yields are 1-2 bps lower. Gold reached a nine-day high near $3345 before falling back to new session lows a little below $3305. July WTI spiked to nearly $64.20 yesterday amid reports that Israel was considering a strike on Iran but reversed slowly and settled near $61.35. Confirmation that OPEC+ was still planning on continuing to boost output may have encouraged follow-through selling today to around $60.40, where the 20-day moving average is found.

USD: The Dollar Index was sold through 99.50 yesterday, which is the (6.18%) retracement of the bounce from the three-year low set April 21 (~97.90). The bounce itself stalled near 102.00, a little ahead of the (6.18%) retracement of the losses from the late March high (~104.70). It is consolidating quietly but firmly between about 99.45 and almost 99.85 so far today. Resistance is seen in the 100.00 area. Still, the five-day moving average will fall back below the 20-day moving average today or tomorrow, and the momentum indicators have begun curling lower. Meanwhile, the fear of financial stress and the cracks beginning to appear in the labor market may become more salient. The US labor market is gradually slowing. The three-month average non-farm payroll increase was below the six-month average for the third month in a row in April. Job growth in the first four months of the year is almost 20% less than the same year ago period. Today's weekly jobless claims may draw some attention. The four-week moving average of weekly initial jobless claims is at its highest level since last October, and the four-week average of continuing claims is at the highest for the year. The cooling of immigration, the government lay-offs, and the weak tourist bookings underscore the risk of continued slowing. Given Fed Chair Powell's contrast of weak survey data and more resilient real sector data, the preliminary May PMI is unlikely to have much impact. April existing home sales are expected to bounce back after falling nearly 6% in March.

Euro: After stalling near the 20-day moving average on Monday and Tuesday, the euro pushed through it (~$1.1280) yesterday and traded above $1.1360. The $1.1380 area is the (61.8%) retracement of the decline from the $1.1575 area seen late April, which was the best level since November 2021. The momentum indicators are turning higher, and the five-day moving average is poised to shortly cross back above the 20-day moving average. But the euro is trading heavier today, and new session lows have been recorded in late European morning turnover near $1.1290. Support is seen in the $1.1265-80 area. The preliminary May PMI disappointed. It showed the eurozone manufacturing continues to contract (49.4 vs. 49.0). It is the fifth consecutive month of improvement, but it has not been above the 50 boom/bust level since June 2022. More disappointing was the service sector. The preliminary PMI fell back below 50 (to 48.9) for the first time since last November. It was the fourth decline in the five months of the year and the largest of the year. The high for the year was set in January (51.3). The composite PMI was also dragged below 50 (to 49.5). It was at 52.2 in May 2024. In Germany, both the manufacturing and services PMI remained below 50 (48.8 and 47.2, respectively) and the composite (output) fell below 50 (48.6), the lowest of the year. Separately, Germany's May IFO survey saw a continued improvement in expectations (highest since last May), but the current assessment weakened for the first time in three months. France's manufacturing and services PMI also remained below 50 even though both edged up. The preliminary composite reading rose to 48.0 from 47.8. That matches this year's high. The market continues to be confident that the ECB will cut rates again when it meets early next month.

CNY: After reaching a six-day high near CNH7.2265 on Tuesday, the dollar's broad weakness pulled it back to slightly below CNH7.20 yesterday. Follow-through selling initially took it to CNH7.1940, a six-day low. It recovered to new session highs near CNH7.2080 in the European morning as the greenback found better traction more broadly. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1903. That was 0.05% lower than yesterday's (CNY7.1937), which is the largest change in over a week, and the lowest dollar fix since April 3. The lower reference rate limits the dollar's upside/yuan's downside.

JPY: The dollar fell for the third consecutive session yesterday and the sixth time in the past seven sessions. It extended its losses initially today to around JPY142.80, a new two-week low. The early denial that US Treasury Secretary Bessent and Japan's Finance Minister Kato did not discuss exchange rate levels, while acknowledging current levels reflected fundamentals, lifted the dollar to session highs near JPY144.40, but the gains were unwound and the greenback was sold to JPY142.80 in early European turnover. It recovered quickly but appears to be stalling around JPY143.50. The momentum indicators have turned down, and the five-day moving average is crossing below the 20-day moving average today. The markets tend not to react much to Japan's PMI, but for the record, the preliminary manufacturing PMI edged up to 49.0 from 48.7. It has not been above 50 since May-June 2024. The services PMI weakened to 50.8 from 52.4. It was at 50.9 at the end of last year. The composite stands at 49.8 (51.2 in April). The sharp sell-off in long-term JGB yields is causing some consternation, but not on Japanese bank shares. The Topix index of bank shares collapsed by 37% from late March through early April, but it has rallied strongly and has overshot the (61.8%) retracement target. It is up almost 40% from the lows. Insurance companies are thought to be more exposed to the very long end of the Japanese curve. The index of Topix insurance companies crashed by more than a quarter in late March-early April swoon. It too recovered smartly but has begun falling again. It was off today for the fourth consecutive session and has lost more than 4% this week. The 40-year bond yield has risen by about 90 bps in the six-week advance coming into this week, and it is up another 22 bps this week. The 30-year yield was up about 65 bps over the past six weeks and is up about 22 bps so far this week.

GBP: Sterling reached a new three-year high yesterday near $1.3470. The five-day moving average crossed above the 20-day moving average on Tuesday, and the momentum indicators have turned higher, but had not reached over-sold territory. A consolidative tone has emerged today. A break of the $1.3370 indicates a near-term corrective phase. The UK's preliminary May manufacturing PMI ticked down to 45.1 from 45.4. It has not been above 50 since last September. The services PMI had slipped below 50 in April (49.0) for the first time since October 2023 but returned to 50.2 in the preliminary May reading. However, the composite remained below 50 (49.4 vs. 48.5) for the first back-to-back sub-50 reading late 2023. The composite was at 53.0 in May 2024.

CAD: The US dollar fell for the third consecutive session against the Canadian dollar, its longest losing streak in a little more than a month. The greenback fell to about CAD1.3815, matching the May 8 low. It is held above CAD1.3845 earlier today and is testing the 20-day moving average in Europe near CAD!.3885. Yesterday's high was near CAD1.3920 and a move above it helps lifted the technical tone. Still, the daily momentum indicators are set to turn lower.

AUD: The Australian dollar fully recovered from the losses suffered at the hands of the dovish central bank. It reached a five day-high yesterday near $0.6470. A trendline off those two highs is near $0.6485 today. It is trading with a softer bias today but within yesterday's range (~$0.6415-$0.6470). It found support at the end of last week and earlier this week slightly below $0.6400, though last week's low was near $0.6355. The momentum indicators are not inspiring, but broadly consistent with the consolidation that has dominated this month. Australia's manufacturing PMI rose every month in Q1 25 before slipping in April to 51.7 and was unchanged in the flash May reading. The services PMI has been eased to 50.5 in May from 51.0 in April. It has not been below 50 since January 2024. The composite PMI ticked down to 50.6 from 51.0 in April. It finished last year at 50.2 and was 52.1 last May.

MXN: The dollar rose against the Mexican peso for only the fourth time this month. We suspect the sharp losses in the US equity market took a toll on the peso. Over the past 30 sessions, changes in the peso are about 0.60 correlated with the S&P 500 and are greater than the G10 currencies. It rivals the Brazilian real, which posted a small gain against the dollar yesterday. The peso's roughly 0.5% loss was the most among emerging market currencies. Mexico updates its Q1 GDP estimate (initially 0.2% quarter-over-quarter) today and the March IGAE economic activity report is due, which is similar to a monthly GDP estimate. They will be overshadowed by the CPI for the first half of May CPI. The year-over-year headline rate may rise above 4% (the upper end of the target range) for the first time this year. The core rate may hold a whisker below the threshold. Given the economic weakness, the firmer CPI is unlikely to impact expectations that Banxico will likely cut the overnight rate by another 50 bp when it meets next on June 26.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: If I Could Only Buy 2 High-Yield REITs Today
data.description:
REITs (VNQ) have been in the market's penalty box ever since early 2022 when the Fed began hiking rates aggressively and long-term rates rose in tandem as fears about inflation remaining higher for longer have pushed rates higher.

Growing concerns about runaway deficit spending and the fiscal implications that it has for the U.S. and ultimately the bond market are driving interest rates higher as well. As a result, REITs, due to their interest rate sensitivity as bond proxies and heavily leveraged entities, have been suffering.

That being said, as I discussed recently, I think that now is a pretty opportune time to invest in the sector. This is because, first of all, they tend to trade at large discounts to the private market value of their real estate. Additionally, most REITs have become increasingly conservative about their balance sheets given the higher-for-longer interest rate environment and therefore have deleveraged, termed-out debt maturities, and are overall in much stronger financial shape than they were just a few years ago.

Finally, I think that certain sectors of real estate are a pretty good place to be right now with growing geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainties. Sectors like triple-net lease with high-quality tenants are pretty resistant to recession as well as a potential flare-up of war in the Middle East or Far East, which could weaken markets and potentially even international trade. Additionally, with the ongoing AI boom, many industries and businesses will be disrupted, but service, industrial, and government-focused triple-net lease properties will likely be quite resilient, especially if they have investment-grade counterparties with long-duration leases.

With this in view, in this article, I am going to share the two high-yield REITs that I would pick if I had to buy just two right now. Note that the definition of high yield used by Seeking Alpha is at least 6%, so any REIT that yields below 6% I am not including in this definition, even though some of my highest conviction REITs at the moment yield less than 6%, so I will not include them in this article.

High-Yield REIT #1

The first REIT on my list is W. P. Carey (WPC). There are several reasons why I like it a lot. First of all, it has a strong balance sheet with a BBB+ credit rating from S&P. It also recently worked through some significant debt maturities, as well as its office disposition program, which now leaves it with a significant amount of liquidity and greatly reduced interest rate headwinds from refinancing debt. It has very little debt (only 1.7% of its total debt) maturing in 2025 and—while it has a large chunk of debt maturing in 2026–2028, the vast majority of that debt is Euro denominated bonds. For context, it recently refinanced a large chunk of its Euro-denominated debt at an all-in rate of 2.8%, so refinancing this portion of its debt is unlikely to pose a major headwind to its bottom line. This gives a lot more certainty to the liability side of its equation.

Additionally, its stock price currently trades at a slight premium to its net asset value, which, I think, is a sweet spot to buy a triple-net lease REIT at because it means that it can access equity markets if it needs to on a reasonably accretive basis without diluting shareholders too much, while at the same time still having significant potential for valuation multiple expansion. In fact, WPC is positioned to capitalize on this, as it recently filed to issue up to $1.25 billion worth of shares which—at the current price—would be at a 4% premium to NAV, and therefore accretive, even after accounting for the 1–2% issuance fees.

It also has additional sources of liquidity it can tap, including the potential to sell its private storage assets, and it can then reinvest in its core triple-net lease business, its substantial construction loan that should be available for liquidity in the near-term, as well as its publicly traded shares in Lineage (LINE).

On top of that, its triple-net lease business model is very recession-resistant, as was evidenced by its strong performance during both the Great Financial Crisis and the COVID-19 outbreak and corresponding lockdowns.

Additionally, its increasing emphasis on industrial real estate could earn it a valuation multiple expansion in the future, as the market may increasingly recognize it as such and reprice it higher alongside much more richly valued industrial REIT peers like STAG Industrial (STAG). Industrial real estate should also benefit from the reshoring of manufacturing to the United States, as there will be a greater need for production and warehouse facilities as a result.

Another reason to really like WPC is simply that its total return potential is the most attractive it has been in quite some time. For one, there is the potential for valuation multiple expansion, as evidenced by the fact that its P/NAV is at a clear discount to its own historical average as well as that of peers like Agree Realty (ADC) and Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT). It also offers a very attractive 6% dividend yield and trades at a discount on a price-to-AFFO basis relative to its own history as well as many peers, including Realty Income (O). Last, but not least, it is expected to grow its dividend per share and FFO per share, per analyst consensus estimates, at a near 5% CAGR over the next several years.

Last but not least, WPC is one of the best-positioned triple-net lease REITs to deal with a higher-for-longer inflationary environment due to the fact that 50% of its rent comes from CPI-linked leases, thereby delivering strong organic growth whenever inflation remains higher. All told, this positions WPC to deliver low teens annualized total return per year without even taking into account the potential for valuation multiple expansion, while also delivering relatively low long-term risk due to its inflation and recession resistance.

High-Yield REIT #2

The second high-yield REIT that I really like right now is Easterly Government Properties (DEA). DEA's stock price has had a very rough go of things recently that culminated in a pretty steep dividend cut earlier this year, and as a result, is in Mr. Market's penalty box.

This is very understandable given that as a triple-net lease REIT that prides itself on having extremely low-risk counterparties—namely the U.S. government, as well as some state governments and related agencies—it is viewed as a leveraged bond proxy. As a result, with interest rates shooting higher, DEA has slumped sharply lower. Additionally, you would think that an investment in DEA, if nothing else, would deliver a very stable stream of passive income given the strength of the counterparties and the conservative nature of the business model. Therefore, when they had to cut it, it sent a really poor signal to Mr. Market.

Finally, there have been a lot of headlines recently about the Department of Government Efficiency slashing government employee headcount and even disrupting entire agencies such as USAID. However, this seems to be a lot more hype than actual reality, as DOGE has underperformed its own projections thus far, Elon Musk is now out of the picture for the most part as he is now refocusing his attention on his various companies, and the current "big, beautiful bill" being negotiated in Congress appears highly likely to actually increase spending moving forward rather than slashing spending. On top of that, the vast majority of DEA's rent comes from leases that the government cannot terminate (a 97% occupancy rate with an 8.5-year weighted average remaining lease term that cannot be terminated by the government).

Therefore, while the market has been punishing DEA substantially for its recent dividend cut, the underlying cash flow quality of the business has remained quite stable. In fact, since the stock priced began crashing hard in 2022 until the present, FFO per share has only declined at a 2% CAGR despite the headwinds that the company has been facing from rising interest rates.

Additionally, this cut now puts the dividend on a much more sustainable path as it enables DEA to retain a lot of cash flow that it can use to sustain its investment-grade credit rating by giving more optionality in dealing with upcoming debt maturities while also retaining sufficient equity to capitalize on new investment opportunities to further diversify the portfolio and grow FFO per share. The expected 2025 FFO payout ratio is just 60% and the expected 2025 AFFO payout ratio is 75%. Moreover, it has no debt maturing in 2025 and just 10% of its debt maturing in 2026, most of which is a term loan at a fairly high interest rate which should not provide much of an earnings headwind to refinance, especially if the Fed cuts rates later this year as the market currently expects since term loans are generally floating rate in nature.

When you combine all that with its very attractive dividend yield of 8.6%, which should be sustainable for the foreseeable future, with a growth profile that should be at least at 3% or higher per year moving forward, the total return profile looks highly compelling from a simple yield plus growth perspective. As management stated on its latest earnings call:

We're focused on long-term growth of 2% to 3%, which we think is very achievable. With that additional capital, I think we're going to be pushing, I think for this call, we'll say, to the upper end of that growth range.

And we're also trying to build a portion of the portfolio that, leads us to have sort of 60 to 100 basis points of escalating growth in the portfolio over time. So we can continue to get those long-term growth rates, to be even higher.

On top of that, shares trade at a huge 37% discount to the private market value of its real estate, despite historically trading close to or even at a premium to its underlying NAV.

Last, but not least, the significant recession resistance of its portfolio due to its counterparties being the U.S. government and state government agencies, makes it an attractive place to allocate capital during these uncertain times. Also, keep in mind that DEA's portfolio is overwhelmingly concentrated in built-to-suit, highly specialized properties that have high replacement costs and are unlikely to be targeted by DOGE.

As such, DEA has a very viable path to delivering double-digit annualized total returns, a very high percentage of which will come through a sustainable and predictable dividend, while also having the potential for significant valuation multiple upside as the negative headlines and concerns around DOGE begin to dissipate in the coming months and years.

Risks and Investor Takeaway

While I like both WPC and DEA a lot for the reasons mentioned in this article, a big risk to keep in mind with both of them is the fact that they are very interest rate sensitive. This is not only because they have significant leverage on their balance sheets and therefore, whenever they have to refinance maturing debt with interest rates—or long-term interest rates currently at elevated levels relative to recent figures—it will likely cause a headwind to FFO per share, but also because they, as triple-net lease REITs, tend to generate long-dated bond-like cash flows. Therefore, whenever interest rates on long-term bonds rise, it decreases the relative value of WPC's and DEA's cash flow and dividend yields, thereby suppressing their share prices.

This is not only a risk in the sense that it reduces near-term total returns because that ultimately does not concern us too much given that we have a long-term investing orientation. If anything, we like it because it means you can buy more shares and therefore get a higher cash flow yield on our investment while we wait for cycles to eventually work themselves out and interest rates to come down. But the more significant risk that comes with this is that it suppresses the growth rate of the REIT by preventing it from being able to issue additional equity on an accretive basis.

While we do not think that this is a huge problem right now for the reasons stated in this article—particularly because they have already cut their dividends recently, so they have a substantial amount of retained cash flow to use to invest in growth projects—it is still something to monitor and keep an eye on, as a prolonged period of elevated interest rates will likely lead to underperformance in terms of growth for both of these REITs.

That said, because of their defensive positioning and their very attractive dividend yield, which should be sustainable for years to come, we think both of these are great opportunities for investors who want a combination of high and sustainable current yield and the potential to achieve significant outperformance over the long term. These are the sorts of opportunities that we target at High Yield Investor.

data.title: AMZY: Collecting Income Where It Isn't Usually Possible
data.description:
Overview

Many of the YieldMax ETFs offer such massive distribution yields that they are difficult to pass up. The general rule of thumb that I follow is that if I held an underlying position in the common shares of a company that one of the YieldMax ETFs aimed to track, I would initiate a small position. Since I held shares of Amazon (AMZN) at the time, I also started a position in YieldMax AMZN Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:AMZY). AMZY offers a very large annual distribution rate of more than 65%, supported by a synthetic option strategy. A large distribution rate like this comes with some unconventional benefits and risks that investors should consider.

I previously covered AMZY and issued a buy rating. Since my last coverage, we've seen some diverse market conditions, which provides us with a little insight into the fund's potential. Following the recent market volatility, I wanted to revisit this option ETF to provide some updated thoughts and insights into its utility and appeal going forward. Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that the price has suffered from a severe decline of 31.6%. When including all distributions that were paid over the same time frame, the total return jumps up to 7.4%.

So, is the high yield offered worth the higher risk profile? I, personally, believe the risk can be worth it, but there are some caveats to that statement. At first glance, there are some clear risks that you can probably spot. However, there are some benefits that a fund like AMZY offers that may not be as obvious if you are looking at this ETF through a traditional lens. AMZY has a gross expense ratio of 0.99% and has net assets of approximately $256M.

Fund Strategy

AMZY deploys a synthetic option strategy with two goals in mind. The first is to replicate the daily price movement of Amazon and the second goal is to harness the implied volatility levels in order to generate option premiums. These option premiums are what funds all of the distributions paid out by the fund. However, the term 'synthetic' means that AMZY doesn't actually own any of the underlying shares of Amazon.

Looking at the holdings below, we can see that AMZY holds some US Treasuries and active call options. This can significantly increase the risk profile of the fund in terms of volatility and price stability. Since AMZY doesn't actually hold shares of Amazon, there can be a large price return discrepancy between the two.

In order to simulate the daily price movement of Amazon, AMZY simultaneously buys call options and sells put options. AMZY implements OTM (out-of-the-money) options and typically selects a strike price that is set between 5% to 15% above the current market price of Amazon. The inclusion of an option strategy naturally limits the upside price potential up to the selected strike price. The fund's prospectus clearly lays out the rules and strategy around the option strategy that I would suggest reading through for further clarity.

Just to demonstrate the vulnerability of the strategy, we can see how AMZY's price compared to AMZN's price over the last six months. When looking at the price movement of the two lined up against one another, it tells a story of the overall vulnerability of the fund. Since the upside is capped due to the option strategy, AMZY is only capable of partially participating in upside movement.

However, we can see how AMZY experiences a more dramatic downside whenever market momentum is negative. Most recently, markets declined in the first quarter of 2025 due to President Trump's tariff proposals. Both AMZY and AMZN declined, but AMZN's price rapidly recovered over recent weeks. Since AMZY's upside is capped, the ETF is unlikely to ever experience the same sort of recovery over time.

Risk and Comparison

The severe price downside that can be experienced is ultimately the main risk of a long-term position in AMZY. Since YieldMax has launched so many different option ETFs that use a similar strategy, there are tons of examples to pull from. Many of these YieldMax funds have shared a similar fate of the price and NAV deteriorating over time, which reinforces that idea that there are inherent tradeoffs when a fund pays a distribution rate this large. Here are some of the most popular YieldMax funds and the respective equities they aim to replicate the movement of.

  • YieldMax MSFT Option Income ETF (MSFO) - Microsoft (MSFT)
  • YieldMax COIN Option Income ETF (CONY) - Coinbase (COIN)
  • YieldMax TSLA Option Income ETF (TSLY) - Tesla (TSLA)
  • YieldMax AMD Option Income ETF (AMDY) - Advanced Micro Devices (AMD)
  • YieldMax META Option Income ETF (FBY) - Meta Platforms (META)
  • YieldMax NVDA Option Income ETF (NVDY) - Nvidia (NVDA)
  • YieldMax MSTR Option Income ETF (MSTY) - Strategy (MSTR)
  • YieldMax NFLX Option Income ETF (NFLY) - Netflix (NFLX)

The performance of these option ETFs is obviously tied to the fact that each tracks a different underlying asset. As a result, MSTY and NFLY are the two outliers here that still managed to provide a positive price return over the last year. Seeing that some of these funds experienced declines greater than 50%, investors should question whether or not a risk of this kind is worth the income potential.

The possibility for large declines like this, is why specifically stated that YieldMax funds are best utilized as accompanying positions. These option ETFs will not be the most efficient way to achieve the highest total return possible. There are many instances where a YieldMax fund has underperformed the very same asset that it aims to track. However, AMZY has done a great job at providing a similar total return that stays on par with AMZN since the ETF's inception.

Based on the performance and strategy of AMZY so far, here are some possible outcomes that will happen based on the future of AMZN.

  1. If the share price of Amazon rapidly increases, AMZY will likely underperform, since the upside potential is capped.
  2. If Amazon trades sideways or sees slower growth, there is a potential for AMZY to outperform. The combination of capital appreciation and high distributions can lead to more favorable results.
  3. If Amazon slowly declines over time, so will AMZY's price. However, there is the potential for AMZY's income to offset the price declines. The caveat is that a lower price and net asset value may also result in lower distribution totals.
  4. As most recently experienced, if Amazon's price rapidly decreases, AMZY's price will as well. The capped upside will make it difficult and unlikely for AMZY to be able to reach the prior price levels before the decline.

Strategic Dividend Goal

Despite these risks, I still maintain optimistic about the utility of the ETF. With many of these YieldMax ETFs that offer a distribution rate over 50%, I believe that the end goal should be to reach 'house money' status. I classify house money status as the cross point where the distributions received are equivalent to or outpace the amount of capital initially invested. While this is unconventional, it's certainly a possibility. For instance, AMZY went public at around $20 per share. Since then, here is the total amount of distributions that have been paid out:

  • 2023: $2.1178 per share paid out in distributions.
  • 2024: $9.0116 per share paid out in distributions.
  • 2025 YTD: $2.6502 per share paid out in distributions
  • Grand Total: $13.7796 per share paid out in distribution since inception.

So if you invested $10,000 into AMZY at the time of inception, which is equivalent to ~500 shares, you would have now received ~$6,889 back in distributions. Since 2024 was the only full year that we can reference, we can estimate that the actual annual distribution rate sits around 45%. From this perspective, we can assume that an investor can get their initial invested capital back in about two and a half years. Investors should be aware that just because the math makes sense, there are still risks that can change the pace of AMZY's returns and overall appeal.

For instance, the dividend payouts can vary month to month and are dependent on market conditions and the overall success of the underlying option strategy. The general trend seems to be that AMZY's distributions are a lot lower when Amazon's share price is trending downward. We can see below that the payouts in the first quarter of 2025 were lower than their usual levels. Coincidentally, this is when market indexes were in decline.

Another part of the appeal of AMZY's large distributions is the tax-efficiency. AMZY strategically issues out ROC (return of capital) distributions, which aren't technically considered income. ROC is generally when a fund gives you back your own capital in the form of dividend, rather than cash that was earned from the underlying business or fund. ROC distributions don't have any income taxes associated, but they do reduce an investor's cost basis. So if you have an average cost basis of $10 and a fund pays a $1 ROC distribution, your cost basis is now reduced down to $9.

If you maintain a position long enough, you can technically reach a point where the cost basis is reduced down to $0 per share. At that point, all distributions received will be classified as long term capital gains. Looking at the most recent payout for May, nearly 95% of the distribution was made up of ROC. Therefore, there's the potential that AMZY can be efficiently utilized within a brokerage account with very little tax consequences.

I am fairly optimistic about the future of Amazon, which is why I continue to maintain a long-term position. My optimism has become stronger since President Trump exempted tech from reciprocal tariffs as well. I believe that one of the most anticipated drivers of growth over the next decade will be the Artificial Intelligence innovations that Amazon continues to build on and invest in.

The main source of involvement is through different channels within AWS (Amazon Web Services), which helps other companies build and expand upon their own AI tools. Precedence Research estimates that the size of the AI market can increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 19.20% through 2034 and Amazon is uniquely positioned to participate in the growth of the market. Amazon currently earns a strong buy rating from both Seeking Alpha's Quant system and Wall Street analysts. However, this growth doesn't happen without its fair share of speed bumps and barriers. For instance, we already saw how markets negatively reacted at the start of the year when DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) launched a similar service to ChatGPT at a fraction of the cost. AMZY can harness these moments of volatility driven by doubt and reward shareholders over the next decade.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on AMZY as the fund has proven to deliver consistent distributions throughout different market environments. Although the synthetic strategy comes with its own set of risks and tradeoffs, there are still plenty of benefits that income investors may be able to enjoy. For instance, early investors of AMZY are well on their way to achieving 'house money', which can be a unique and unconventional approach to these YieldMax funds. I believe that AMZY is best utilized as an accompanying fund, rather than a core position because of the high probability of underperforming over a longer time horizon.

data.title: Endava: Priced For Stagnation Or Obsolescence
data.description:
Introduction

I recently updated my view on Globant (GLOB), which saw its stock crash 30% after a modest miss and lowered guidance. This resembles what has happened to Endava (NYSE:DAVA), and I decided to take a deeper look at this company's operations and valuation. As in many cases for IT consultants, it seems that DAVA has been hit by a buyer's strike first due to AI, which caused clients/companies to stop and reassess how to spend IT budgets and now the Trump tariff tantrum that threw many companies into crisis mode and placed IT decision on the back burner or in the trash can.

The Growth Challenge

The company experienced a sharp decline in revenue starting in 2Q23, sharply contrasting with its more than 20-year history. This was due to AI technology breakthroughs that forced many companies to reassess how to implement AI, what to do with current IT projects, who and how much to budget for new contracts. This buyer's strike lasted most of 2023. It was starting to see trend normalization when the tariff crisis hit and threw companies into crisis mode, causing delays in new contracts or cancellations of existing ones.

DAVA, Globant, EPAM (EPAM), etc. have highly stable business models, where contracts provide visibility for revenue and earnings. The AI and now tariff crisis threw a massive wrench into this visibility and forced companies to downgrade guidance to what current contracts are slated to generate revenue. New contracts, i.e., growth, were taken off the table.

In 1Q25 (fiscal 3Q25), DAVA reported zero revenue growth compared to 1Q24 but posted a sharp gain in margins, which was due to reduced headcount or productivity. AI has been a positive factor for IT consultants; they can utilize technology to write code, etc.

When Can Growth Resume

Looking at the consensus quarterly revenue forecast, the market has implemented DAVA's guidance and assumes almost flat revenue for the remainder of calendar 2025. Green shoots are expected in 2Q26, and a new top-line growth expectation of under 10% for fiscal 2027 is also expected.

While these revenue estimates seem low, the company has demonstrated that it can maintain or improve margins, which leads to an EBITDA growth of 100% in 2025 and around 10% in the 2026/27 time frame. My preferred metric, cash earnings or CE (net income, depreciation + stock-based compensation), may grow around 11%. The bar has been set very low for DAVA.

More importantly, with valuation severely impacted, they will be unlikely to execute significant acquisitions, resulting in over US$100m in free cash flow that can shore up the balance sheet or be used to buy back shares.

Valuation: Priced for Stagnation

This is the good part of DAVA. The stock has fallen hard since 2024, down 80%, leaving it at a deep discount of 6.2x P/CE or 0.5x PEG on fiscal 2025 estimates. The market has thrown in the towel and expects the company to stagnate or shrink into obscurity. The risk-reward equation is compelling. If DAVA can show modest revenue and earnings growth in the next few quarters, the stock can recover.

The consensus price target for fiscal 2026 (June 2026) is US$25 or 10x P/CE at a .9x PEG. I agree that DAVA does not warrant a higher multiple yet and value the stock at 1x PEG or US$27 for FY26, which is over 90% upside potential.

Peer Comps

I compiled a comparison table using consensus data that measures growth and valuation for a wide range of peers and competitors. DAVA stands out as one of the cheapest on a relative or PEG metric. The large caps look expensive, and most suffer the same AI and tariff hits.

Risk

The sector is highly competitive, and in an economic slowdown, some companies may elect to price services/contracts lower, which may impact Endava's current and future margins as well as growth.

Conclusion

I rate DAVA a buy. The stock has been deeply discounted to the point that it incorporates no growth or obsolescence, providing a compelling risk-reward equation. The first sign of revenue/contract growth may start a rally with 90% upside potential.
